title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
This document covers two foundational pillars of classical time series analysis — decomposition and ARIMA modelling — with side-by-side Python and R code throughout.

Environment note: Python chunks are executed live (via jupyter: python3). R equivalents are shown in fenced r blocks for reference. To run R, switch the YAML engine to knitr and rename {python} chunks to {r}.

Topics covered:

Setup & data
Visualisation
Classical decomposition (additive & multiplicative)
STL decomposition
Stationarity testing (ADF & KPSS)
ACF & PACF
ARIMA — theory, manual fitting, diagnostics
Auto-ARIMA
SARIMA — seasonal extension
Forecasting & model comparison

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
Python

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.dates as mdates

from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller, kpss
from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal import seasonal_decompose, STL
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA
from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error
from scipy.stats import probplot, norm

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.dpi": 120,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.3,
})

print("Python ready.")

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
Simulated Dataset
We simulate 10 years of monthly retail sales with trend, seasonality, and noise.

np.random.seed(42)
n = 120
t = np.arange(n)

trend    = 200 + 2.5 * t
seasonal = 50 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 12) + 20 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 6)
noise    = np.random.normal(0, 15, n)
sales    = trend + seasonal + noise

dates = pd.date_range(start="2014-01-01", periods=n, freq="MS")
df = pd.DataFrame({"sales": sales}, index=dates)

# Train / test split — hold out last 12 months
train = df["sales"][:-12]
test  = df["sales"][-12:]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 4))
ax.plot(df.index, df["sales"], linewidth=1.5, color="steelblue")
ax.axvline(test.index[0], color="crimson", linestyle="--", label="Train / Test split")
ax.set_title("Simulated Monthly Retail Sales", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Date")
ax.set_ylabel("Sales (units)")
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.YearLocator())
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter("%Y"))
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(df.describe().round(2))

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA

R Equivalent

         rnorm(n, 0, 15)

# Base ts object — used by forecast package
ts_sales <- ts(sales, start = c(2014, 1), frequency = 12)

train_r <- window(ts_sales, end   = c(2022, 12))
test_r  <- window(ts_sales, start = c(2023,  1))

plot(ts_sales, main = "Simulated Monthly Retail Sales",
     ylab = "Sales", xlab = "Date", col = "steelblue")

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
Rolling Statistics
Rolling mean and standard deviation reveal non-stationarity: a rising mean confirms a trend, growing spread suggests heteroscedasticity.

roll_mean = df["sales"].rolling(12).mean()
roll_std  = df["sales"].rolling(12).std()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(11, 6), sharex=True)

axes[0].plot(df.index, df["sales"],  alpha=0.5,  label="Observed",    color="steelblue")
axes[0].plot(df.index, roll_mean,    linewidth=2, label="12-mo Mean",  color="crimson")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Sales")
axes[0].legend()
axes[0].set_title("Rolling Statistics", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")

axes[1].plot(df.index, roll_std, color="darkorange", label="12-mo Std Dev")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Std Dev")
axes[1].legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
3.1 Additive Decomposition

decomp_add = seasonal_decompose(df["sales"], model="additive", period=12)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(11, 10), sharex=True)
parts = {
    "Observed" : (decomp_add.observed,  "steelblue"),
    "Trend"    : (decomp_add.trend,     "crimson"),
    "Seasonal" : (decomp_add.seasonal,  "darkorange"),
    "Residual" : (decomp_add.resid,     "purple"),
}
for ax, (label, (data, color)) in zip(axes, parts.items()):
    ax.plot(data, color=color, linewidth=1.2)
    ax.set_ylabel(label, fontsize=10)
    if label == "Residual":
        ax.axhline(0, linestyle="--", color="gray", alpha=0.5)

axes[0].set_title("Classical Additive Decomposition", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
R Equivalent
decomp_add <- decompose(ts_sales, type = "additive")
plot(decomp_add)

# Access components
decomp_add$trend
decomp_add$seasonal
decomp_add$random

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
3.2 Multiplicative Decomposition

decomp_mult = seasonal_decompose(df["sales"], model="multiplicative", period=12)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(11, 10), sharex=True)
parts_m = {
    "Observed" : (decomp_mult.observed,  "steelblue"),
    "Trend"    : (decomp_mult.trend,     "crimson"),
    "Seasonal" : (decomp_mult.seasonal,  "darkorange"),
    "Residual" : (decomp_mult.resid,     "purple"),
}
for ax, (label, (data, color)) in zip(axes, parts_m.items()):
    ax.plot(data, color=color, linewidth=1.2)
    ax.set_ylabel(label, fontsize=10)
    if label == "Residual":
        ax.axhline(1, linestyle="--", color="gray", alpha=0.5)  # residual ~ 1 in multiplicative

axes[0].set_title("Classical Multiplicative Decomposition", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
R Equivalent
decomp_mult <- decompose(ts_sales, type = "multiplicative")
plot(decomp_mult)

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
3.3 STL Decomposition (Seasonal-Trend via Loess)
STL is more flexible than classical decomposition:

Handles changing seasonality over time
Robust to outliers (with robust=True)
Works for any seasonal period
Always additive (log-transform the series first for multiplicative behaviour)


stl = STL(df["sales"], period=12, robust=True)
stl_res = stl.fit()

fig = stl_res.plot()
fig.set_size_inches(11, 9)
fig.suptitle("STL Decomposition (Robust)", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", y=1.01)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Seasonal strength: 0 = no seasonality, 1 = perfectly seasonal
var_resid    = np.var(stl_res.resid)
var_seasonal = np.var(stl_res.seasonal + stl_res.resid)
strength = max(0, 1 - var_resid / var_seasonal)
print(f"Seasonal strength F_S = {strength:.4f}  (>0.64 = strong)")

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
R Equivalent
stl_r <- stl(ts_sales, s.window = "periodic", robust = TRUE)
plot(stl_r, main = "STL Decomposition (Robust)")

# Seasonal strength via feasts
tsibble_sales |> features(sales, feat_stl)

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
4.1 Augmented Dickey-Fuller

def adf_report(series, name):
    res = adfuller(series.dropna(), autolag="AIC")
    verdict = "STATIONARY ✓" if res[1] < 0.05 else "NON-STATIONARY ✗"
    print(f"\nADF — {name}")
    print(f"  Statistic : {res[0]:.4f}")
    print(f"  p-value   : {res[1]:.4f}  →  {verdict}")
    print(f"  Lags used : {res[2]}")
    for k, v in res[4].items():
        print(f"  Critical {k}: {v:.4f}")

adf_report(df["sales"],        "Original series")
adf_report(df["sales"].diff(), "First difference")

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
R Equivalent
library(tseries)
adf.test(ts_sales)          # original
adf.test(diff(ts_sales))    # first difference

# More control via urca
library(urca)
summary(ur.df(ts_sales, type = "drift", selectlags = "AIC"))

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
4.2 KPSS Test

def kpss_report(series, name):
    stat, pval, lags, crits = kpss(series.dropna(), regression="c", nlags="auto")
    verdict = "NON-STATIONARY ✗" if pval < 0.05 else "STATIONARY ✓"
    print(f"\nKPSS — {name}")
    print(f"  Statistic : {stat:.4f}")
    print(f"  p-value   : {pval:.4f}  →  {verdict}")
    print(f"  Lags used : {lags}")

kpss_report(df["sales"],        "Original series")
kpss_report(df["sales"].diff(), "First difference")

R Equivalent

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
Theory
ARIMA(p, d, q) combines three components:

AR(p) — Autoregressive: \(Y_t\) depends on its own past p values
I(d) — Integrated: series is differenced d times to achieve stationarity
MA(q) — Moving Average: \(Y_t\) depends on past q forecast errors

\[
\phi(B)(1-B)^d Y_t = \theta(B)\varepsilon_t
\]
where \(B\) is the backshift operator, \(\phi\) are AR coefficients, \(\theta\) are MA coefficients, and \(\varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\).
Selection heuristic:

Determine d from stationarity tests
Read p from PACF (cuts off at lag p)
Read q from ACF (cuts off at lag q)
Compare candidate models by AIC / BIC

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
6.1 Manual Fitting

arima_model = ARIMA(train, order=(2, 1, 2))
arima_fit   = arima_model.fit()
print(arima_fit.summary())

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
R Equivalent
arima_r <- arima(train_r, order = c(2, 1, 2))
summary(arima_r)

# or with forecast package (adds tidy AIC/BIC)
arima_r2 <- Arima(train_r, order = c(2, 1, 2))
summary(arima_r2)

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
6.2 Forecasting with ARIMA

fc_obj  = arima_fit.get_forecast(steps=12)
fc_mean = fc_obj.predicted_mean
fc_ci   = fc_obj.conf_int(alpha=0.05)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5))
ax.plot(train.index[-36:], train[-36:], label="Training",  color="steelblue")
ax.plot(test.index,         test,        label="Actual",   color="black", linewidth=2)
ax.plot(fc_mean.index,      fc_mean,     label="Forecast", color="crimson",
        linestyle="--", linewidth=2)
ax.fill_between(fc_ci.index, fc_ci.iloc[:, 0], fc_ci.iloc[:, 1],
                alpha=0.2, color="crimson", label="95% CI")
ax.set_title("ARIMA(2,1,2) Forecast", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Date")
ax.set_ylabel("Sales")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

mae  = mean_absolute_error(test, fc_mean)
rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(test, fc_mean))
print(f"MAE  = {mae:.2f}")
print(f"RMSE = {rmse:.2f}")

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
R Equivalent
fc_arima_r <- forecast(arima_r2, h = 12)
autoplot(fc_arima_r) +
  autolayer(test_r, series = "Actual", colour = "black") +
  labs(title = "ARIMA(2,1,2) Forecast") +
  theme_minimal()

accuracy(fc_arima_r, test_r)

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
6.3 Residual Diagnostics
A well-fitted model should have residuals that are:

Centred on zero with no trend
Uncorrelated (white noise) — check with Ljung-Box
Approximately normally distributed


residuals = arima_fit.resid.dropna()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 8))

# Residuals over time
axes[0, 0].plot(residuals, color="steelblue", linewidth=0.8)
axes[0, 0].axhline(0, color="crimson", linestyle="--", alpha=0.6)
axes[0, 0].set_title("Residuals Over Time")

# Histogram + normal fit
axes[0, 1].hist(residuals, bins=25, edgecolor="white",
                color="steelblue", density=True)
xr = np.linspace(residuals.min(), residuals.max(), 200)
axes[0, 1].plot(xr, norm.pdf(xr, residuals.mean(), residuals.std()),
                color="crimson", lw=2, label="Normal")
axes[0, 1].set_title("Histogram of Residuals")
axes[0, 1].legend()

# ACF of residuals
plot_acf(residuals, lags=30, ax=axes[1, 0],
         title="ACF of Residuals", zero=False)

# Q-Q plot
probplot(residuals, plot=axes[1, 1])
axes[1, 1].set_title("Q-Q Plot")

plt.suptitle("ARIMA(2,1,2) Residual Diagnostics",
             fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", y=1.01)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Ljung-Box test
lb = acorr_ljungbox(residuals, lags=[10, 20], return_df=True)
print("\nLjung-Box Test (p > 0.05 = no autocorrelation ✓):")
print(lb.round(4))

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
R Equivalent
# One-line diagnostic plot + Ljung-Box
checkresiduals(arima_r2)

# Ljung-Box only
Box.test(residuals(arima_r2), lag = 20, type = "Ljung-Box")

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
6.4 Auto-ARIMA
Instead of reading ACF/PACF manually, auto-ARIMA searches over (p, d, q) combinations and selects by AIC.

try:
    from pmdarima import auto_arima
    auto_model = auto_arima(
        train,
        seasonal=False,
        d=None,                       # auto-detect d
        information_criterion="aic",
        stepwise=True,
        trace=True,
        error_action="ignore",
        suppress_warnings=True,
    )
    print(f"\nSelected order : {auto_model.order}")
    print(f"AIC            : {auto_model.aic():.2f}")
except ImportError:
    print("Install pmdarima:  pip install pmdarima")

title: Time Series Analysis: Classical Decomposition & ARIMA
text:
7.1 Fitting SARIMA

sarima_model = SARIMAX(
    train,
    order=(1, 1, 1),
    seasonal_order=(1, 1, 1, 12),
    enforce_stationarity=False,
    enforce_invertibility=False,
)
sarima_fit = sarima_model.fit(disp=False)
print(sarima_fit.summary())

fc_s_mean = fc_s.predicted_mean

                alpha=0.2, color="seagreen", label="95% CI")
ax.set_title("SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)[12] Forecast", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("Date")
ax.set_ylabel("Sales")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

mae_s  = mean_absolute_error(test, fc_s_mean)
rmse_s = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(test, fc_s_mean))
print(f"MAE  = {mae_s:.2f}")
print(f"RMSE = {rmse_s:.2f}")

title: Regression Analysis with statsmodels
text:
Regression analysis is one of the most widely used statistical techniques. In Python, statsmodels provides R-style formula interfaces, detailed summary tables, and a rich set of diagnostic tools — making it the go-to library for inferential regression.
This document covers:

Simple and multiple OLS regression
Interpreting coefficients and the summary table
Model diagnostics (residuals, normality, homoscedasticity, multicollinearity)
Polynomial and interaction terms
Logistic regression

title: Regression Analysis with statsmodels
text:
2.1 Core Concept
Regression analysis is a statistical method used to model and quantify the relationship between a dependent variable (outcome) and one or more independent variables (predictors). At its heart, regression answers the question: “How does Y change when X changes, holding everything else constant?”
The most fundamental form is Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) linear regression, which fits a straight line (or hyperplane in multiple dimensions) through observed data by minimising the sum of squared residuals — the vertical distances between each data point and the fitted line:
\[
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}\right)^2
\]

title: Regression Analysis with statsmodels
text:
2.2 Types of Regression








Type
Use Case
Example




Simple Linear
One predictor, continuous outcome
mpg ~ wt


Multiple Linear
Several predictors, continuous outcome
mpg ~ wt + hp + cyl


Polynomial
Non-linear curvature in one predictor
mpg ~ wt + I(wt²)


Interaction
Effect of X₁ depends on X₂
mpg ~ wt * am


Logistic
Binary outcome (0/1)
am ~ mpg + wt


Poisson / GLM
Count or proportion data
claims, rates

title: Regression Analysis with statsmodels
text:
2.3 The Four OLS Assumptions (LINE)
For OLS estimates to be unbiased and efficient (BLUE — Best Linear Unbiased Estimator), four assumptions must hold:

Linearity — the relationship between predictors and outcome is linear in parameters.
Independence — observations are independent of each other (no autocorrelation).
Normality — the residuals \(\varepsilon_i\) are approximately normally distributed.
Equal variance (Homoscedasticity) — the spread of residuals is constant across all fitted values.

Violating these assumptions doesn’t necessarily invalidate the model, but it does affect the reliability of standard errors, p-values, and confidence intervals. The diagnostics section of this document tests each assumption explicitly.

title: Regression Analysis with statsmodels
section: 2.4 What Regression Can and Cannot Do
text:
2.4 What Regression Can and Cannot Do






ImportantCorrelation ≠ Causation



Regression quantifies association, not causation. A statistically significant coefficient means the predictor is linearly related to the outcome in the sample — it does not prove that X causes Y. Causal claims require additional design considerations (randomisation, instrumental variables, etc.).


Regression can:

Estimate the average change in Y per unit change in X, controlling for other variables
Quantify uncertainty in estimates via standard errors and confidence intervals
Test hypotheses about individual coefficients (t-test) or the model as a whole (F-test)
Predict new outcomes given predictor values

Regression cannot:

Establish causality from observational data alone
handle predictors that are perfectly correlated (multicollinearity causes the design matrix to be singular)
Extrapolate reliably far beyond the range of the training data

title: Regression Analysis with statsmodels
text:
3.1 Advantages of Python
1. General-purpose ecosystem Python is a full-stack programming language. The same environment used for regression can handle data ingestion, API calls, web scraping, building dashboards (Dash, Streamlit), deploying machine learning models, and automating pipelines — with no context switching.
2. Machine learning integration Python’s scikit-learn, XGBoost, LightGBM, and PyTorch ecosystems are the industry standard for predictive modelling. When a project evolves from inference (statsmodels) toward prediction and deployment, everything stays in one language.
3. Industry adoption and job market Python consistently ranks as the most popular language in data science hiring. Teams in tech, finance, and consulting typically standardise on Python, making code sharing and collaboration easier.
4. Performance and scalability Libraries like NumPy, Polars, and GPU-backed frameworks make Python well-suited for large datasets that would strain R’s in-memory model.
5. Software engineering practices Python has mature tooling for testing (pytest), packaging (pip/poetry), version control workflows, and containerisation (Docker) — essential when regression code moves into production.
6. Quarto and notebook support Python integrates seamlessly with Quarto, Jupyter, and VS Code, enabling reproducible, publication-quality documents (like this one).

title: Regression Analysis with statsmodels
text:
3.2 Disadvantages of Python (Compared to R)
1. Statistical output is less rich out of the box R’s lm() and related functions were purpose-built by statisticians. summary(lm(...)) in R prints p-values, significance stars, and diagnostic cues with a single call. statsmodels matches most of this, but the ecosystem for niche statistical methods (mixed models, survival analysis, Bayesian hierarchical models) is generally more mature in R.
2. Formula syntax is a secondary concern R’s formula interface (y ~ x1 + x2) is native to the language. In Python, statsmodels adds it as a convenience layer (smf.ols("y ~ x1 + x2")), but base Python has no concept of formulas — this can feel bolted-on for statisticians.
3. Tidyverse-style data wrangling R’s tidyverse (dplyr, tidyr, ggplot2) provides an exceptionally coherent and readable grammar for data manipulation and visualisation. pandas and seaborn cover similar ground but with less syntactic consistency.
4. Academic statistics community defaults to R Most econometrics, biostatistics, and social science textbooks and journals use R. Replicating published analyses often requires translating R code, which adds friction.
5. Verbose setup for simple tasks Running a quick regression in R takes three lines. In Python, you typically import five libraries and manage more boilerplate before reaching the model.

TipWhen to Use Which



Choose Python when your work sits within a broader data engineering or ML pipeline, your team is mixed (engineers + analysts), or you need to deploy models as services. Choose R when your work is primarily statistical inference, your audience is academic, or you heavily rely on specialised statistical packages. Many practitioners use both — Python for pipelines and deployment, R for exploratory analysis and publication figures.

1.4 Functions

Functions let you reuse code. Define once with def, call many times. Use return to send a value back.

1.5 Data Structures

Python has four built-in data structures. Choosing the right one matters for both clarity and performance.













1.5.1 Lists — Ordered & Mutable
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "date"]

# Indexing — 0-based!
print(fruits[0])     # first element

print(fruits[1:3])   # middle slice

Important🔢 0-Based Indexing — The #1 Gotcha for R Users!



List:   ["apple",  "banana",  "cherry",  "date"]

R:          [1]       [2]        [3]       [4]    ← starts at 1
The first element in Python is always [0]. This catches every R programmer when they first learn Python!


List Comprehensions — the Pythonic, compact way to build lists:
# [expression for item in iterable if condition]

2 A Simple Python Program: Data Structures in Action






Note📋 What This Program Covers



This is a beginner-friendly program that shows how Python’s four core data structures work together in a real scenario — tracking students and their course data. No external libraries needed — just pure Python.


We will model a small class of students using all four data structures you learned:













Step 1 — Set Up the Data
# ── Step 1: Use a TUPLE for fixed course information ──────────────
# Tuples are immutable — course details don't change mid-semester
course_info = ("Data Science 101", "DSC101", 2025, "Semester 1")
course_name, course_code, year, semester = course_info

print(f"Course : {course_name} ({course_code})")
print(f"Period : {semester}, {year}")
Course : Data Science 101 (DSC101)
Period : Semester 1, 2025






Tip💡 Why a Tuple here?



Course details are fixed — they should never accidentally be changed. A tuple enforces that: course_info[0] = "New Name" would raise a TypeError.





Step 2 — Store Student Records
# ── Step 2: Use a DICTIONARY for each student's record ────────────
# Dict maps student ID → their data
students = {
    "S001": {"name": "Alice",   "grades": [85, 92, 78, 90], "year": 2},
    "S002": {"name": "Bob",     "grades": [70, 65, 80, 72], "year": 1},
    "S003": {"name": "Carol",   "grades": [95, 98, 92, 97], "year": 3},
    "S004": {"name": "David",   "grades": [60, 55, 70, 65], "year": 1},
    "S005": {"name": "Eve",     "grades": [88, 84, 91, 87], "year": 2},
}

# Access a specific student
alice = students["S001"]
print(f"Student : {alice['name']}")
print(f"Grades  : {alice['grades']}")

title: Python for Data Science
text:
1.4 Functions

Functions let you reuse code. Define once with def, call many times. Use return to send a value back.













1.4.1 Basic Functions
def greet(name):
    """Return a greeting message."""
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

print(greet("Alice"))
print(greet("Bob"))
Hello, Alice!
Hello, Bob!


1.4.2 Default Arguments & *args / **kwargs


# Default argument values
def power(base, exponent=2):
    return base ** exponent

print(power(3))       # uses default
print(power(3, 3))    # override default
print(power(2, 10))
9
27
1024
# *args — extra positional args
def total(*args):
    return sum(args)

print(total(1, 2, 3))
print(total(10, 20, 30, 40))
6
100

# **kwargs — keyword arguments
def describe(**kwargs):
    for key, value in kwargs.items():
        print(f"  {key}: {value}")

describe(name="Alice",
         age=30,
         city="Colombo")
  name: Alice
  age: 30
  city: Colombo




1.4.3 Scope & Docstrings
x = "global"

def show_scope():
    x = "local"    # different variable!
    print(f"Inside: x = '{x}'")

show_scope()
print(f"Outside: x = '{x}'")
Inside: x = 'local'
Outside: x = 'global'


1.4.4 Lambda Functions






Note💡 What is a Lambda?



A lambda is a tiny, anonymous, one-line function. Use it when you need a quick function without defining a full def block.


# Regular function vs lambda — same result
def square(x):
    return x ** 2

square_lambda = lambda x: x ** 2

print(square(5))          # 25
print(square_lambda(5))   # 25

# Lambda shines inside other functions
numbers = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]
sorted_desc = sorted(numbers, key=lambda x: -x)
print(sorted_desc)
25
25
[9, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 1]






Tip🆚 Python vs R — Functions




Comparison TablePython CodeR Code





Feature
Python
R




Define
def f(x):
f <- function(x) {


Return
Explicit return x
Last expression auto-returned


Anonymous
lambda x: x*2
function(x) x*2


Default args
def f(x=10):
function(x=10)


Docstring
"""text"""
#' @description (roxygen2)





def add(a, b=10):
    """Add two numbers."""
    return a + b

print(add(5))     # 15
print(add(5, 3))  # 8


add <- function(a, b = 10) {
  a + b   # auto-returned!
}

add(5)    # [1] 15
add(5, 3) # [1] 8




Key difference: In R, the last expression is automatically returned — no return keyword needed. Python always requires an explicit return. Both approaches are valid; Python’s is more explicit.

title: Python for Data Science
text:
1.5 Data Structures

Python has four built-in data structures. Choosing the right one matters for both clarity and performance.













1.5.1 Lists — Ordered & Mutable
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "date"]

# Indexing — 0-based!
print(fruits[0])     # first element
print(fruits[-1])    # last element

# Slicing [start:stop:step]
print(fruits[1:3])   # middle slice
print(fruits[::2])   # every other

# Modify
fruits.append("elderberry")
fruits.insert(1, "avocado")
fruits.remove("banana")
print(fruits)
apple
date
['banana', 'cherry']
['apple', 'cherry']
['apple', 'avocado', 'cherry', 'date', 'elderberry']






Important🔢 0-Based Indexing — The #1 Gotcha for R Users!



List:   ["apple",  "banana",  "cherry",  "date"]
Python:     [0]       [1]        [2]       [3]    ← starts at 0
R:          [1]       [2]        [3]       [4]    ← starts at 1
The first element in Python is always [0]. This catches every R programmer when they first learn Python!


List Comprehensions — the Pythonic, compact way to build lists:
# [expression for item in iterable if condition]
squares      = [x**2 for x in range(1, 11)]
even_squares = [x**2 for x in range(1, 11) if x % 2 == 0]

print(squares)
print(even_squares)
[1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100]
[4, 16, 36, 64, 100]


1.5.2 Tuples — Ordered & Immutable
# Parentheses — cannot change after creation
coordinates = (40.7128, -74.0060)   # lat, lon of New York
print(coordinates[0])

# Tuple unpacking — elegant!
lat, lon = coordinates
print(f"Latitude: {lat}, Longitude: {lon}")
40.7128
Latitude: 40.7128, Longitude: -74.006


1.5.3 Dictionaries — Key-Value Pairs
student = {
    "name":     "Alice",
    "age":      22,
    "grades":   [88, 92, 79],
    "enrolled": True
}

# Access
print(student["name"])            # direct access
print(student.get("gpa", "N/A")) # safe access with default

# Add / update
student["university"] = "UoM"
student["age"] = 23

# Iterate
for key, value in student.items():
    print(f"  {key:12} → {value}")
Alice
N/A
  name         → Alice
  age          → 23
  grades       → [88, 92, 79]
  enrolled     → True
  university   → UoM


1.5.4 Sets — Unique Values
# Duplicates are removed automatically
numbers = {1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4}
print(numbers)

# Set operations
a = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
b = {4, 5, 6, 7, 8}

print("Union:       ", a | b)
print("Intersection:", a & b)
print("Difference:  ", a - b)
{1, 2, 3, 4}
Union:        {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8}
Intersection: {4, 5}
Difference:   {1, 2, 3}






Tip🆚 Python vs R — Data Structures




Comparison TablePython CodeR Code










Python
R Equivalent
Notes




list
list()
R lists can be named; Python lists are simpler


tuple
No direct equivalent
R has no immutable sequence


dict
named list()
d["key"] in Python vs lst$key in R


set
unique() + intersect()
R has no set type; uses functions instead


[x**2 for x in lst]
sapply(lst, function(x) x^2)
List comprehension vs apply family





# List
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4]
doubled = [x * 2 for x in nums]

# Dict
info = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30}
print(info["name"])


# Vector
nums <- c(1, 2, 3, 4)
doubled <- sapply(nums, function(x) x * 2)

# Named list
info <- list(name = "Alice", age = 30)
info$name    # or info[["name"]]

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
Pandas is an open-source Python library built on top of NumPy for fast, flexible data manipulation and analysis. Created by Wes McKinney in 2008, it is the de facto standard for tabular data work in Python.
Its two core data structures are:

Series — a 1-D labeled array (like a spreadsheet column)
DataFrame — a 2-D labeled table (like an Excel sheet or SQL table)

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
3 The Series Object
A Pandas Series is a 1-D labeled array. The R equivalent is a named vector.

3.1 Creating

PythonR


# From a list (auto integer index)
s = pd.Series([0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0])
print(s)
#> 0    0.25
#> 1    0.50
#> 2    0.75
#> 3    1.00
#> dtype: float64
# From a dict (named index)
population = pd.Series({
    'California': 38332521,
    'Texas':      26448193,
    'New York':   19651127
})
print(population)
#> California    38332521
#> Texas         26448193
#> New York      19651127
#> dtype: int64


# Unnamed vector
s <- c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0)
s
#> [1] 0.25 0.50 0.75 1.00
# Named vector (≈ dict-based Series)
population <- c(California = 38332521,
                Texas      = 26448193,
                "New York" = 19651127)
population
#> California      Texas   New York
#>   38332521   26448193   19651127





3.2 Indexing & Slicing








Operation
Python
R




By label
population['California']
population["California"]


By position
population.iloc[0]
population[1] (1-indexed)


Slice (label)
population['California':'Texas'] (inclusive)
population[c("California","Texas")]


Slice (position)
s[0:2] (excludes end)
s[1:2] (includes end)


Boolean
s[s > 0.5]
s[s > 0.5]


Names / Index
population.index
names(population)




PythonR


print(population['California'])        # by label
print(population.iloc[0])             # by position
print(population['California':'Texas'])# label slice (both ends inclusive)
print(s[s > 0.5])                     # boolean mask
#> 38332521
#> 38332521
#> California    38332521
#> Texas         26448193
#> dtype: int64
#> 2    0.75
#> 3    1.00
#> dtype: float64


population["California"]              # by label
population[1]                         # by position (1-indexed)
population[c("California","Texas")]   # label slice
s[s > 0.5]                           # boolean mask
#> California
#>   38332521
#> California
#>   38332521
#> California      Texas
#>   38332521   26448193
#> [1] 0.75 1.00









Important



Indexing difference: Python is 0-based; R is 1-based. Python label slices are inclusive on both ends; positional slices exclude the end. R slices are always inclusive.

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
4 The DataFrame Object
A DataFrame is a 2-D labeled table — rows and columns both have labels.

4.1 Creating

PythonR


area = pd.Series({'California': 423967, 'Texas': 695662,
                  'New York': 141297,   'Florida': 170312})
pop  = pd.Series({'California': 38332521, 'Texas': 26448193,
                  'New York': 19651127,   'Florida': 19552860})

states = pd.DataFrame({'population': pop, 'area': area})
print(states)
#>             population    area
#> California    38332521  423967
#> Texas         26448193  695662
#> New York      19651127  141297
#> Florida       19552860  170312


states <- data.frame(
  population = c(California=38332521, Texas=26448193,
                 "New York"=19651127, Florida=19552860),
  area       = c(California=423967,   Texas=695662,
                 "New York"=141297,   Florida=170312)
)
states
#>            population   area
#> California   38332521 423967
#> Texas        26448193 695662
#> New York     19651127 141297
#> Florida      19552860 170312





4.2 Essential Operations








Operation
Python
R




Column access
states['area']
states$area


Add column
states['density'] = states['population'] / states['area']
states$density <- states$population / states$area


Dimensions
states.shape → (4, 2)
dim(states) → 4 2


Column names
states.columns
colnames(states)


Row names
states.index
rownames(states)


Transpose
states.T
t(states)




PythonR


# Add a derived column
states['density'] = states['population'] / states['area']
print(states)
print("\nshape:", states.shape)
#>             population    area     density
#> California    38332521  423967   90.413926
#> Texas         26448193  695662   38.018740
#> New York      19651127  141297  139.076746
#> Florida       19552860  170312  114.806121
#>
#> shape: (4, 3)


# Add a derived column
states$density <- states$population / states$area
states
cat("dim:", dim(states), "\n")
#>            population   area   density
#> California   38332521 423967  90.41393
#> Texas        26448193 695662  38.01874
#> New York     19651127 141297 139.07675
#> Florida      19552860 170312 114.80612
#>
#> dim: 4 3





4.3 Constructing from Other Sources

PythonR


# From list of dicts — missing keys become NaN
pd.DataFrame([{'a': 1, 'b': 2}, {'b': 3, 'c': 4}])
#>      a  b    c
#> 0  1.0  2  NaN
#> 1  NaN  3  4.0
# From NumPy array
np.random.seed(42)
pd.DataFrame(np.random.rand(3, 2), columns=['foo', 'bar'])
#>         foo       bar
#> 0  0.374540  0.950714
#> 1  0.731994  0.598658
#> 2  0.156019  0.155995


# From list — missing values become NA
data.frame(a=c(1,NA), b=c(2,3), c=c(NA,4))
#>    a b  c
#> 1  1 2 NA
#> 2 NA 3  4
# From matrix
set.seed(42)
matrix(runif(6), nrow=3, ncol=2) |>
  as.data.frame() |> setNames(c("foo","bar"))
#>         foo       bar
#> 1 0.9148060 0.8304476
#> 2 0.9370754 0.6417455
#> 3 0.2861395 0.5190959

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
5 Data Indexing & Selection

5.1 Three Ways to Index a DataFrame (Python)








Indexer
Selects by
Example




df['col']
Column label
states['area']


df.loc[row, col]
Label (both axes)
states.loc['Texas', 'area']


df.iloc[row, col]
Integer position (both axes)
states.iloc[1, 1]



R uses df[row, col] for both — strings for labels, integers for positions.


5.2 Position-based & Label-based

PythonR


# First 3 rows, first 2 columns — position-based
states.iloc[:3, :2]
#>             population    area
#> California    38332521  423967
#> Texas         26448193  695662
#> New York      19651127  141297
# Label-based: specific row + column
states.loc['Texas', 'area']
#> 695662


# First 3 rows, first 2 columns — position-based
states[1:3, 1:2]
#>            population   area
#> California   38332521 423967
#> Texas        26448193 695662
#> New York     19651127 141297
# Label-based: specific row + column
states["Texas", "area"]
#> [1] 695662





5.3 Boolean Masking & Column Selection

PythonR


# Rows where density > 100, select two columns
states.loc[states['density'] > 100, ['population', 'density']]
#>           population     density
#> New York    19651127  139.076746
#> Florida     19552860  114.806121


# Base R
states[states$density > 100, c("population", "density")]
#>          population  density
#> New York   19651127 139.0767
#> Florida    19552860 114.8061
# dplyr (more readable for complex filters)
states %>% filter(density > 100) %>% select(population, density)
#>          population  density
#> New York   19651127 139.0767
#> Florida    19552860 114.8061









Important



Key difference: Python’s loc (label) and iloc (position) are explicit separate indexers. R uses [row, col] for both — the type of value (string vs integer) determines the mode.

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
6 Operating on Data

6.1 NumPy UFuncs Preserve Labels
Pandas operations preserve index and column labels automatically.

PythonR


ser = pd.Series([6, 3, 7, 4])
np.exp(ser)           # index preserved
#> 0     403.428793
#> 1      20.085537
#> 2    1096.633158
#> 3      54.598150
#> dtype: float64


ser <- c(6, 3, 7, 4)
round(exp(ser), 4)    # names preserved if named
#> [1]  403.4288   20.0855 1096.6332   54.5982





6.2 Automatic Index Alignment
This is one of Pandas’ most powerful features — and one R does not do automatically.

PythonR


area = pd.Series({'Alaska': 1723337, 'Texas': 695662, 'California': 423967})
pop  = pd.Series({'California': 38332521, 'Texas': 26448193, 'New York': 19651127})

pop / area    # unmatched keys fill with NaN — automatically
#> Alaska              NaN
#> California    90.413926
#> New York            NaN
#> Texas         38.018740
#> dtype: float64


area <- c(Alaska=1723337, Texas=695662, California=423967)
pop  <- c(California=38332521, Texas=26448193, "New York"=19651127)

# R does NOT auto-align — manual intersection required
common <- intersect(names(pop), names(area))
pop[common] / area[common]
#> California      Texas
#>   90.41393   38.01874









Tip



Pandas fill value trick: Use A.add(B, fill_value=0) to treat missing index entries as 0 rather than NaN when adding two Series.




6.3 Arithmetic Method Equivalents



Python operator
Pandas method
R




+
.add(fill_value=)
+


-
.sub()
-


*
.mul()
*


/
.div()
/


//
.floordiv()
%/%


**
.pow()
^

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
7 Handling Missing Data

7.1 How Missing Values Are Represented




Python (Pandas)
R




General
np.nan / None
NA


Float
np.nan (IEEE 754)
NA_real_


Integer
⚠️ Upcasts column to float64
NA_integer_ (native)


String
None
NA_character_









Important



R has typed NAs — an integer column stays integer even with missing values. Pandas converts integer columns to float64 when any NaN is introduced.



PythonR


# Integer array becomes float64 when NaN added
vals = np.array([1, np.nan, 3, 4])
print("dtype:", vals.dtype)

s = pd.Series([1, np.nan, 2, None])
print(s)
#> dtype: float64
#> 0    1.0
#> 1    NaN
#> 2    2.0
#> 3    NaN
#> dtype: float64


# Integer stays integer with NA
vals <- c(1L, NA_integer_, 3L, 4L)
cat("class:", class(vals), "\n")

s <- c(1, NA, 2, NA)
print(s)
#> class: integer
#> [1]  1 NA  2 NA





7.2 Detecting, Dropping & Filling

PythonR


df = pd.DataFrame([[1, np.nan, 2],
                   [2, 3,      5],
                   [np.nan, 4, 6]])

# Detect
print(df.isnull())

# Drop rows with ANY missing
print(df.dropna())

# Drop cols with ANY missing
print(df.dropna(axis='columns'))

# Fill with constant
print(df.fillna(0))

# Forward / back fill
s = pd.Series([1, np.nan, 2, np.nan, 3], index=list('abcde'))
print(s.ffill())
print(s.bfill())
#>        0      1      2
#> 0  False   True  False
#> 1  False  False  False
#> 2   True  False  False
#>
#>      0    1  2
#> 1  2.0  3.0  5
#>
#>    2
#> 0  2
#> 1  5
#> 2  6
#>
#>      0    1  2
#> 0  1.0  0.0  2
#> 1  2.0  3.0  5
#> 2  0.0  4.0  6
#>
#> a    1.0
#> b    1.0
#> c    2.0
#> d    2.0
#> e    3.0
#> dtype: float64
#> a    1.0
#> b    2.0
#> c    2.0
#> d    3.0
#> e    3.0
#> dtype: float64


df <- data.frame(V1=c(1,2,NA), V2=c(NA,3,4), V3=c(2,5,6))

# Detect
print(is.na(df))

# Drop rows with ANY missing
print(na.omit(df))

# Drop cols with ANY missing
print(df[, colSums(is.na(df)) == 0, drop=FALSE])

# Fill with constant
df_fill <- df; df_fill[is.na(df_fill)] <- 0
print(df_fill)

# Forward / back fill (tidyr)
data_fill <- c(a=1, b=NA, c=2, d=NA, e=3)
print(fill(data.frame(x=data_fill), x, .direction="down")$x)
print(fill(data.frame(x=data_fill), x, .direction="up")$x)
#>       V1    V2    V3
#> [1,] FALSE  TRUE FALSE
#> [2,] FALSE FALSE FALSE
#> [3,]  TRUE FALSE FALSE
#>
#>   V1 V2 V3
#> 2  2  3  5
#>
#>   V3
#> 1  2
#> 2  5
#> 3  6
#>
#>   V1 V2 V3
#> 1  1  0  2
#> 2  2  3  5
#> 3  0  4  6
#>
#> [1] 1 1 2 2 3
#> [1] 1 2 2 3 3

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
8 Hierarchical Indexing
Pandas MultiIndex stores multi-level labels on rows or columns. R uses a long-format data frame with grouping columns and tidyr for reshaping.

PythonR


# Create MultiIndex Series
index = pd.MultiIndex.from_product(
    [['California','New York','Texas'], [2000, 2010]],
    names=['state', 'year'])
pop = pd.Series(
    [33871648, 37253956, 18976457, 19378102, 20851820, 25145561],
    index=index)
print(pop)
#> state       year
#> California  2000    33871648
#>             2010    37253956
#> New York    2000    18976457
#>             2010    19378102
#> Texas       2000    20851820
#>             2010    25145561
#> dtype: int64
# Partial index & unstack → wide table
print(pop['California'])          # one state
print(pop.unstack())              # long → wide
#> year
#> 2000    33871648
#> 2010    37253956
#> dtype: int64
#>
#> year            2000      2010
#> state
#> California  33871648  37253956
#> New York    18976457  19378102
#> Texas       20851820  25145561


# Equivalent: long-format data frame
pop <- data.frame(
  state      = rep(c("California","New York","Texas"), each=2),
  year       = rep(c(2000, 2010), times=3),
  population = c(33871648,37253956,18976457,19378102,20851820,25145561))
print(pop)
#>        state year population
#> 1 California 2000   33871648
#> 2 California 2010   37253956
#> 3   New York 2000   18976457
#> 4   New York 2010   19378102
#> 5      Texas 2000   20851820
#> 6      Texas 2010   25145561
# Filter one group (≈ partial index)
pop %>% filter(state == "California")

# Long → wide (≈ unstack)
pop %>% pivot_wider(names_from=year, values_from=population)
#>        state year population
#> 1 California 2000   33871648
#> 2 California 2010   37253956
#>
#>        state     2000     2010
#> 1 California 33871648 37253956
#> 2   New York 18976457 19378102
#> 3      Texas 20851820 25145561









Tip



unstack() / stack() in Pandas ↔︎ pivot_wider() / pivot_longer() in tidyr — same concept, different syntax.

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
9 Combining Datasets: Concat & Append

9.1 pd.concat() — the one function for all stacking

PythonR


def make_df(cols, ind):
    return pd.DataFrame(
        {c: [str(c)+str(i) for i in ind] for c in cols}, ind)

df1 = make_df('AB', [1,2]); df2 = make_df('AB', [3,4])
df3 = make_df('AB', [0,1]); df4 = make_df('CD', [0,1])

# Row-wise (default axis=0)
print(pd.concat([df1, df2]))
#>     A   B
#> 1  A1  B1
#> 2  A2  B2
#> 3  A3  B3
#> 4  A4  B4
# Column-wise (axis=1)
print(pd.concat([df3, df4], axis=1))
#>     A   B   C   D
#> 0  A0  B0  C0  D0
#> 1  A1  B1  C1  D1
# Mismatched columns — outer join fills NaN (default)
df5 = make_df('ABC',[1,2]); df6 = make_df('BCD',[3,4])
print(pd.concat([df5, df6]))          # outer (default)
print(pd.concat([df5, df6], join='inner'))  # inner: shared cols only
#>      A   B   C    D
#> 1   A1  B1  C1  NaN
#> 2   A2  B2  C2  NaN
#> 3  NaN  B3  C3   D3
#> 4  NaN  B4  C4   D4
#>
#>     B   C
#> 1  B1  C1
#> 2  B2  C2
#> 3  B3  C3
#> 4  B4  C4
# Label source rows with keys → creates MultiIndex
print(pd.concat([df1, df2], keys=['x','y']))
#>       A   B
#> x 1  A1  B1
#>   2  A2  B2
#> y 3  A3  B3
#>   4  A4  B4


df1 <- data.frame(A=c("A1","A2"), B=c("B1","B2"))
df2 <- data.frame(A=c("A3","A4"), B=c("B3","B4"))
df3 <- data.frame(A=c("A0","A1"), B=c("B0","B1"))
df4 <- data.frame(C=c("C0","C1"), D=c("D0","D1"))

# Row-bind (≈ axis=0 concat)
rbind(df1, df2)
#>    A  B
#> 1 A1 B1
#> 2 A2 B2
#> 3 A3 B3
#> 4 A4 B4
# Column-bind (≈ axis=1 concat)
cbind(df3, df4)
#>    A  B  C  D
#> 1 A0 B0 C0 D0
#> 2 A1 B1 C1 D1
# Mismatched columns — bind_rows fills NA; rbind() would error
df5 <- data.frame(A=c("A1","A2"), B=c("B1","B2"), C=c("C1","C2"))
df6 <- data.frame(B=c("B3","B4"), C=c("C3","C4"), D=c("D3","D4"))
dplyr::bind_rows(df5, df6)        # outer (fills NA)
#>      A  B  C    D
#> 1   A1 B1 C1 <NA>
#> 2   A2 B2 C2 <NA>
#> 3 <NA> B3 C3   D3
#> 4 <NA> B4 C4   D4
# Inner — keep shared columns only
shared <- intersect(names(df5), names(df6))
rbind(df5[, shared], df6[, shared])
#>    B  C
#> 1 B1 C1
#> 2 B2 C2
#> 3 B3 C3
#> 4 B4 C4
# Source labelling (≈ keys=)
df1$source <- "x"; df2$source <- "y"
dplyr::bind_rows(df1, df2)
#>    A  B source
#> 1 A1 B1      x
#> 2 A2 B2      x
#> 3 A3 B3      y
#> 4 A4 B4      y









Warning



rbind() vs bind_rows(): Base R rbind() errors on mismatched columns. dplyr::bind_rows() fills missing columns with NA, matching pd.concat() outer-join behaviour.








Note



DataFrame.append() is gone. It was deprecated in Pandas 1.4 and removed in Pandas 2.0. Always use pd.concat().

title: Computation on NumPy Arrays
text:
1 Universal Functions (UFuncs)
Computation on NumPy arrays can be very fast, or it can be very slow. The key to making it fast is to use vectorized operations — applying an operation to the whole array at once instead of looping through each element.

These vectorized operations are implemented through NumPy’s universal functions. Any time you see a loop over an array in Python, think about whether a ufunc can replace it.




2 Array Arithmetic
NumPy uses Python’s standard arithmetic operators directly on arrays. Each operator applies to every element at once.

NumPy (Python)Base R




Code
import numpy as np

x = np.arange(4)
print("x      =", x)
print("x + 5  =", x + 5)
print("x - 2  =", x - 2)
print("x * 3  =", x * 3)
print("x / 2  =", x / 2)
print("x // 2 =", x // 2)   # floor division
print("x ** 2 =", x ** 2)   # exponentiation
print("x % 2  =", x % 2)    # modulus



// is floor division (drops the decimal), ** is power, % is the remainder.





Code
x <- 0:3
x <- 0:3
print(x + 5)
print(x - 2)
print(x * 3)
print(x / 2)
print(x %/% 2)  # floor division
print(x ^ 2)     # exponentiation
print(x %% 2)    # modulus



The operators are nearly identical, with minor syntax differences: ^ instead of **, %/% instead of //.







3 Useful Math Functions
Beyond basic arithmetic, NumPy provides functions for absolute values, trigonometry, exponentials, and logarithms — all applied element-wise.

NumPy (Python)Base R




Code
import numpy as np

# Absolute value
x = np.array([-3, -1, 0, 1, 3])
print("abs:", np.abs(x))

# Trigonometry
theta = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 3)
print("sin:", np.sin(theta).round(2))
print("cos:", np.cos(theta).round(2))

# Exponentials and logarithms
x = np.array([1, 2, 3])
print("e^x:    ", np.exp(x).round(3))
print("ln(x):  ", np.log(x).round(3))
print("log2(x):", np.log2(x).round(3))






Code
# Absolute value
x <- c(-3, -1, 0, 1, 3)
print(abs(x))

# Trigonometry
theta <- seq(0, pi, length.out = 3)
print(round(sin(theta), 2))
print(round(cos(theta), 2))

# Exponentials and logarithms
x <- c(1, 2, 3)
print(round(exp(x), 3))
print(round(log(x), 3))
print(round(log2(x), 3))



3.0.1 What Happened?

R’s abs(), sin(), cos(), exp(), log() and log2() work exactly the same way.
The function names are almost identical.








4 Aggregations
After doing computations, you often want to summarise an array into a single value — the sum, mean, min, or max.

NumPy (Python)Base R




Code
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(0)
arr = np.random.randint(1, 100, size=6)
print("Array:", arr)

print("sum:   ", arr.sum())
print("min:   ", arr.min())
print("max:   ", arr.max())
print("mean:  ", arr.mean().round(2))
print("std:   ", arr.std().round(2))
print("median:", np.median(arr))



You can call them as np.sum(arr) or as a method directly on the array: arr.sum() — both work.
Always prefer arr.sum() over Python’s built-in sum(arr) on NumPy arrays — the NumPy version is much faster.





Code
set.seed(0)
arr <- sample(1:99, 6, replace = TRUE)
cat("Array:", arr, "\n")

print(arr)
print(sum(arr))
print(min(arr))
print(max(arr))
print(round(mean(arr), 2))
print(round(sd(arr), 2))
print(median(arr))



4.0.1 What Happened?

R’s aggregation functions are identical in concept: sum(), min(), max(), mean(), sd(), median().
The main difference is that in NumPy you can call them as methods on the array itself (arr.mean()), which R does not do.








5 NumPy vs R: Computation Quick Reference



Task
NumPy (Python)
Base R




Add scalar
x + 5
x + 5


Power
x ** 2
x ^ 2


Floor division
x // 2
x %/% 2


Modulus
x % 2
x %% 2


Absolute value
np.abs(x)
abs(x)


Sine / Cosine
np.sin(x) / np.cos(x)
sin(x) / cos(x)


Natural log
np.log(x)
log(x)


Exponential
np.exp(x)
exp(x)


Sum
arr.sum()
sum(arr)


Min / Max
arr.min() / arr.max()
min(arr) / max(arr)


Mean
arr.mean()
mean(arr)


Standard deviation
arr.std()
sd(arr)


Median
np.median(arr)
median(arr)




If you already know R, NumPy computation will feel very familiar. The operators and function names are almost the same — the biggest difference is that in NumPy you can call aggregations as methods directly on the array (arr.mean()).

title: Introduction to Python for Data Science & Statistics
text:
1.1 History and Origin

1.1.1 Guido van Rossum and the Birth of Python



Guido van Rossum — Creator of Python (photo: Dan Stroud, CC BY)





👨‍💻

Created by
Guido van Rossum



📅

First release
February 1991



🏛️

Origin
CWI, Netherlands



🎭

Named after
Monty Python comedy


Python was created by Guido van Rossum, a Dutch programmer, who began work on it in December 1989 at Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI) in the Netherlands. The first public release — Python 0.9.0 — appeared in February 1991.






Note🎭 Why the name “Python”?



The name has nothing to do with the snake. Van Rossum was a fan of Monty Python’s Flying Circus and wanted a short, unique, slightly quirky name. The snake branding came much later — and stuck!



Key milestones in Python’s history:












1.1.2 Python 2 vs Python 3






Important⛔ Always Use Python 3 — No Exceptions



Python 2 reached End-of-Life on 1 January 2020. All major scientific libraries (NumPy, pandas, SciPy, scikit-learn) have dropped Python 2 support. Install Python 3 — there is absolutely no reason to install Python 2 for new work.










Feature
Python 2 ❌ (retired)
Python 3 ✅ (use this)




print
print "hello"
print("hello")


Integer division
5 / 2 → 2
5 / 2 → 2.5


Strings
ASCII by default
Unicode (UTF-8) everywhere


range()
Returns a full list
Returns memory-efficient iterator


input()
Use raw_input()
input() works correctly


Status
☠️ Dead (Jan 2020)
✅ Actively developed






1.1.3 Interpreted Language — What Does That Mean for Statistics?
Python is an interpreted language — code runs line-by-line, just like R. This is ideal for statistical work:



⚡

Interactive

Code
# Run this line-by-line in any Python interpreter or Jupyter cell
import math
data       = [12.4, 15.6, 10.8, 14.2]
mean_val   = sum(data) / len(data)
variance   = sum((x - mean_val)**2 for x in data) / len(data)
std_approx = math.sqrt(variance)
print(f"Mean: {mean_val:.2f}   SD: {std_approx:.2f}")
# → Mean: 13.25   SD: 1.81

1.2 Key Features of Python

1.2.1 📖 Readable and Simple Syntax
Python enforces indentation (4 spaces) instead of braces {}. Code reads like structured English — critical when statisticians who are not software engineers need to read and verify the code.


Code
# ── Compute a one-sample t-statistic from scratch ─────────────────
# Anyone can follow this, even without a Python background
sample   = [22.1, 19.8, 23.4, 21.0, 20.5]
n        = len(sample)
mean     = sum(sample) / n
variance = sum((x - mean)**2 for x in sample) / (n - 1)   # Bessel's correction
std_err  = (variance / n) ** 0.5

t_stat   = (mean - 20) / std_err    # H₀: population mean = 20
print(f"n={n}   mean={mean:.2f}   t={t_stat:.3f}")

text:
2.3 pip and conda — Package Managers

2.3.1 What is a Package Manager?
A package manager is like an app store for Python libraries — it installs, updates, and removes packages and their dependencies automatically.



🐍

pip
Official Python manager, installs from PyPI



🅲

conda
Anaconda’s manager, handles compiled libraries



🏪

PyPI
500,000+ packages at pypi.org



🌿

conda-forge
Community-maintained conda packages





2.3.2 conda — The Anaconda Way
conda manages both packages and environments, and handles non-Python compiled dependencies (C/Fortran libraries) that pip can’t always handle.


Code
conda search scipy                           # Find a package
conda install scipy                          # Install into current environment
conda install scipy=1.11.0                   # Install specific version
conda install numpy pandas matplotlib seaborn  # Install multiple packages
conda update scipy                           # Update one package
conda update --all                           # Update everything
conda remove scipy                           # Remove a package
conda list                                   # Show all installed packages
conda env list                               # Show all environments




2.3.3 pip — The Python Standard Manager


Code
pip install statsmodels                      # Install from PyPI
pip install statsmodels==0.14.0             # Specific version
pip install -r requirements.txt             # Install from a requirements file
pip install --upgrade statsmodels           # Upgrade
pip uninstall statsmodels                   # Remove
pip list                                    # List all installed
pip show numpy                              # Details about one package




2.3.4 conda vs pip — Decision Table






Warning⚠️ Best Practice: conda first, pip as fallback



Inside a conda environment, prefer conda install. Only use pip for packages not available via conda, and do so after all conda installs. Mixing carelessly can break environments.





Situation
Use




Scientific packages (NumPy, SciPy, pandas)
✅ conda install


Package only on PyPI, not in conda
✅ pip install


Managing isolated project environments
✅ conda create


Compiled C/Fortran extensions
✅ conda preferred


Niche or personal packages
✅ pip install






2.3.5 Virtual Environments — Why They Matter
A virtual environment is an isolated Python installation. Each project gets its own Python version and package versions — they never interfere with each other.











Code
# Create a fresh environment for your statistics project
conda create --name stats_project python=3.11

# Activate it (your prompt changes to: (stats_project) $)
conda activate stats_project

# Install all packages you need FOR THIS PROJECT ONLY
conda install numpy pandas scipy statsmodels matplotlib seaborn jupyter

# When done working, deactivate
conda deactivate

# Delete when no longer needed
conda env remove --name stats_project

section: 3.4 Putting It All Together — Your First Statistical Session
text:
3.4 Putting It All Together — Your First Statistical Session






Note🚀 Complete Workflow: Launch → Analyse → Visualise



Follow these steps to run your first real statistical analysis in Python.



3.4.1 Step 1 — Launch your environment


Code
conda activate stats_project    # Activate your environment
cd ~/Documents/stats_project    # Navigate to your project
jupyter lab                     # Open JupyterLab




3.4.2 Step 2 — Set up your notebook


Code
# Cell 1: Import all libraries
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import scipy.stats as stats
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", palette="deep")
%matplotlib inline
%config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'retina'

print("✅ Libraries loaded successfully!")
print(f"   numpy   {np.__version__}")
print(f"   pandas  {pd.__version__}")




3.4.3 Step 3 — Create data and run a t-test


Code
# Cell 2: Simulate two groups and run an independent t-test
np.random.seed(2024)
control   = np.random.normal(loc=50, scale=10, size=30)
treatment = np.random.normal(loc=56, scale=10, size=30)

t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(control, treatment)
print(f"t-statistic = {t_stat:.3f}")
print(f"p-value     = {p_value:.4f}")
print("→ Statistically significant!" if p_value < 0.05 else "→ Not significant at α = 0.05")




3.4.4 Step 4 — Visualise


Code
# Cell 3: Boxplot + strip plot
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 5))

# Left: Boxplot
axes[0].boxplot([control, treatment], labels=["Control", "Treatment"],
                patch_artist=True,
                boxprops=dict(facecolor="#dbeafe", color="#1d4ed8"),
                medianprops=dict(color="#dc2626", linewidth=2))
axes[0].set_ylabel("Score")
axes[0].set_title(f"Independent t-test\nt = {t_stat:.2f},  p = {p_value:.4f}")

# Right: Distributions
x = np.linspace(20, 90, 300)
for grp, col, lbl in [(control,"#3b82f6","Control"),(treatment,"#ef4444","Treatment")]:
    mu, sd = grp.mean(), grp.std(ddof=1)
    axes[1].plot(x, stats.norm.pdf(x, mu, sd), linewidth=2.5, label=f"{lbl} (μ={mu:.1f})", color=col)
    axes[1].hist(grp, bins=12, density=True, alpha=0.25, color=col)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Score")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Density")
axes[1].set_title("Overlapping Distributions")
axes[1].legend()

plt.suptitle("First Statistical Analysis in Python", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Data Visualization using Matplotlib
text:
To construct any data visualization, both languages require specific core components, but they organize them using completely different workflows.


R builds plots declaratively by stacking independent components layer-by-layer based on the “Grammar of Graphics”. To draw a plot in R, you must combine three essential elements using the plus sign (+): [ = + + ]

Data: The raw data frame container containing your variables.
Aesthetics Mapping (aes): Tells R which variables map to the visual properties of the graph (e.g., matching a column to the X-axis or Y-axis, or assigning group colors).
Geometric Layer (geom_): Specifies the physical shapes used to draw the data points on the screen (such as geom_col() for bars, geom_histogram() for distributions, or geom_point() for scatter coordinates).




Python builds plots imperatively using an object-oriented canvas hierarchy. Instead of linking layers, you execute sequential commands top-down to modify a canvas state:

The Figure Container (plt.figure): The physical, blank page windows container holding your entire visual output.
The Axes Coordinate Plane: The specific grid window plane inside the figure box where numbers map to physical X and Y coordinate scales.
The Plotting Engine (plt.bar, plt.hist, etc.): The explicit execution call that stamps your raw data arrays directly onto that coordinate plane.
The Decoration Methods (plt.title, plt.xlabel): Sequential commands that add titles, grid lines, and axis labels before rendering.
