title: Data Visualization using Matplotlib
section: Deep Dive: How to Draw Plots in Python
text:
Deep Dive: How to Draw Plots in Python
To program visualizations in Python effectively, you must understand how Matplotlib tracks and updates your drawing canvas. Matplotlib offers two primary interfaces for compiling graphics.

1. The Imperative State-Machine Interface (pyplot)
This shortcut interface relies on the global pyplot tracking engine to manage an active, hidden “current state” behind the scenes.

Tracking State: Every time you call a command like plt.bar() or plt.title(), Python asks: “Which figure layout is currently open?” It automatically applies that operation to the active window wrapper.
The Step-by-Step State Lifecycle:

Step A: Call plt.figure() to instantiate and register a clean global canvas state.
Step B: Invoke your rendering engine (e.g., plt.hist()). Python automatically draws the axis grid limits based on your list variables.
Step C: Call mutation methods (plt.xlabel(), plt.grid()). These alter the details of the active state.
Step D: Invoke plt.show(). This flushes the background graphics memory buffer and locks the visual state to display the completed figure.




2. The Object-Oriented Interface (Explicit Canvas Mapping)
For advanced, production-grade applications, developers drop the hidden global state and explicitly capture the individual layout elements as named Python objects.
Instead of hiding the canvas behind the plt wrapper, you explicitly decouple the container window from the interior tracking grid using plt.subplots():
# Unpacking the figure box object and coordinate grid object explicitly
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.5))

# Calling rendering commands directly as object methods
ax.scatter(student_heights, student_weights)

# Mutating properties explicitly on the coordinate axis object
ax.set_title("Bivariate Correlation Analysis")
ax.set_xlabel("Independent Variable: Height (cm)")
By transitioning to object methods like ax.set_xlabel(), you gain low-level, absolute programmatic control over every standalone element on your data dashboard grid layout.

title: Data Visualization using Matplotlib
section: Let’s move on to some practical examples of these statistical plots.
text: Let’s move on to some practical examples of these statistical plots.

title: Data Visualization using Matplotlib
text:
Standard Bar Charts

Statistical Application
Bar charts compare the central tendency (such as sample means) or raw frequencies across discrete categorical variables.


Python Syntax Focus

The Engine: plt.bar(x, height) maps index positions. Python loops over the categorical iterable to assign text labels along the X-axis, while mapping numeric values to column heights on the Y-axis.
Common Trap: Both input sequences must have identical lengths, or Python will throw a ValueError: Incompatible shapes runtime error.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Statistics: Discrete Categorical Variable (Independent Groupings)
beverages = ['Coffee', 'Tea', 'Juice', 'Soda']

# Statistics: Absolute Frequency Distribution (Dependent Numeric Variable)
units_sold = [250, 180, 90, 120]

# Initialization: Instantiating a clean figure container
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4.5))

# Execution: Passing mandatory positional variables to the rendering engine
plt.bar(beverages, units_sold, color=['#4E3629', '#1B4D3E', '#F4A261', '#E63946'])

# Decoration: Adding statistical metadata and labels
plt.title('Categorical Frequency Comparison', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Independent Categorical Variables (Discrete Strings)')
plt.ylabel('Dependent Frequency Metric (Numeric Values)')
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)

plt.show()

title: Data Visualization using Matplotlib
text:
Stacked Bar Charts

Statistical Application
Stacked bar charts compare the total frequency of major groups while simultaneously displaying the proportional breakdown of subgroups within each category.


Python Syntax Focus

The Engine: bottom=vector keyword argument. To stack bars, you call plt.bar() multiple times. The second call must explicitly receive the values of the first call as its baseline starting point.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

categories = ['Store A', 'Store B', 'Store C']
online_sales = [120, 150, 80]
instore_sales = [130, 30, 40]

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4.5))

# Plot baseline layer
plt.bar(categories, online_sales, label='Online', color='#2A9D8F')

# Plot second layer stacked directly on top of the baseline layer
plt.bar(categories, instore_sales, bottom=online_sales, label='In-Store', color='#E76F51')

plt.title('Sales Breakdown by Subgroup (Stacked)', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Retail Outlets')
plt.ylabel('Total Sales Volume')
plt.legend()
plt.show()

title: Data Visualization using Matplotlib
text:
Clustered Bar Charts

Statistical Application
Clustered bar charts place related subgroups side-by-side. This makes it easier to directly compare values across multiple subcategories without looking at an absolute total.


Python Syntax Focus

The Engine: Vector offset mathematics using NumPy. Python requires you to shift the X-coordinates of each subgroup manually using a defined pixel width value.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

categories = ['Q1', 'Q2', 'Q3', 'Q4']
year_2024 = [45, 58, 62, 79]
year_2025 = [52, 64, 61, 85]

# Generate a continuous tracking array for indices
x_indices = np.arange(len(categories))
bar_width = 0.35

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4.5))

# Subtract and add half-width to center clusters perfectly around x ticks
plt.bar(x_indices - bar_width/2, year_2024, width=bar_width, label='2024', color='#264653')
plt.bar(x_indices + bar_width/2, year_2025, width=bar_width, label='2025', color='#F4A261')

plt.title('Quarterly Revenue Growth (Clustered)', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')

plt.xlabel('Fiscal Quarters')
plt.ylabel('Revenue (Millions)')
plt.xticks(x_indices, categories)
plt.legend()
plt.show()

title: Data Visualization using Matplotlib
text:
Line Charts

Statistical Application
Line charts trace the movement and directional trends of continuous variables across a sequential scale, traditionally measured over chronological time intervals.


Python Syntax Focus

The Engine: plt.plot(x, y) tracks and maps elements using continuous line segments. The optional marker parameter allows you to emphasize exact observation coordinates.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Chronological intervals (Months) and continuous observations
months = ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar', 'Apr', 'May', 'Jun']
temperature = [22, 24, 28, 31, 33, 30]

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4.5))

# marker='o' flags exact coordinate indices with solid data points
plt.plot(months, temperature, color='#E76F51', linestyle='-', marker='o', linewidth=2)

plt.title('Chronological Temperature Progression', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Timeline Distribution (Months)')
plt.ylabel('Continuous Measurement (Celsius)')
plt.grid(True, linestyle=':', alpha=0.6)

plt.show()

title: Data Visualization using Matplotlib
text:
Histograms

Statistical Application
Histograms visualize the empirical probability distribution, sample spread, skewness, and modality of a single continuous numerical dataset.


Python Syntax Focus

The Engine: Unlike bar charts, plt.hist(x) accepts only one data array. Python’s backend automatically sweeps the data, locates the range minimum and maximum, and builds mathematical intervals.
Key Argument: The bins parameter accepts an integer to slice the data range into equal-width buckets automatically.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Statistics: A single unorganized sample distribution of a continuous numeric variable
exam_scores = [55, 62, 67, 71, 74, 78, 82, 85, 88, 90, 91, 93, 95, 97, 99]

# Initialization: Open a fresh distinct chart window context
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4.5))

# Execution: 'bins=5' makes Python calculate ranges and divide data automatically
plt.hist(exam_scores, bins=5, color='#2A9D8F', edgecolor='white', linewidth=1.2)

# Decoration: Apply tracking documentation metrics to continuous axis layout
plt.title('Sample Population Probability Distribution', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Continuous Scale Intervals (Statistical Numeric Bins)')
plt.ylabel('Observation Frequency Density (Count per Bin)')

plt.show()

title: Data Visualization using Matplotlib
text:
Box Plots

Statistical Application
Box plots visualize the dispersion, spread, symmetry, and central tendency of a dataset by mapping its Five-Number Summary: Minimum, First Quartile (\(Q_1\)), Median (\(Q_2\)), Third Quartile (\(Q_3\)), and Maximum, while explicitly flagging individual outliers.


Python Syntax Focus

The Engine: plt.boxplot(x) runs background percentile calculations automatically. The box marks the Interquartile Range (IQR), the inner line represents the median, and the outer whiskers extend to \(1.5 \times \text{IQR}\).


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Dataset containing sample values along with extreme outliers
performance_metrics = [42, 51, 55, 57, 59, 60, 62, 63, 65, 66, 68, 72, 75, 94, 98]

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4.5))

# patch_artist=True fills the inner quartiles box canvas container with color
plt.boxplot(performance_metrics, patch_artist=True, 
            boxprops=dict(facecolor='#E9C46A', color='black'),
            medianprops=dict(color='red', linewidth=2))

plt.title('Distribution Descriptive Summary (Box Plot)', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.ylabel('Measurement Scale Range')
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle=':', alpha=0.6)

plt.show()

title: Data Visualization using Matplotlib
text:
Scatter Plots

Statistical Application
Scatter plots diagnose the direction, strength, and structural pattern of the correlation between two paired continuous variables.


Python Syntax Focus

The Engine: plt.scatter(x, y) takes matched vector pairings. It extracts index elements x[i] and y[i] simultaneously to plot exact spatial points on a Cartesian matrix.
Key Argument: The alpha parameter regulates pixel transparency. Setting it below 1.0 helps expose data overplotting, allowing users to visually spot high-density clusters.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Statistics: Two paired continuous numerical measurements for correlation mapping
student_heights = [150, 160, 165, 170, 175, 180, 185]
student_weights = [50, 58, 62, 65, 74, 78, 85]

# Initialization: Set up clear dimensional window properties
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4.5))

# Execution: 's' changes marker pixels; 'alpha' adds transparency to map dense data clusters
plt.scatter(student_heights, student_weights, color='#E76F51', s=120, edgecolors='black', alpha=0.85)

# Decoration: Decorate matrix with statistical reference grids
plt.title('Bivariate Correlation Analysis', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.xlabel('Independent Explanatory Variable: Height (cm)')
plt.ylabel('Dependent Response Variable: Weight (kg)')
plt.grid(True, linestyle=':', alpha=0.6)

plt.show()

title: Data Visualization using Matplotlib
text:
Pie Charts

Statistical Application
Pie charts show relative frequencies and part-to-whole nominal percentages. They should only be used when individual subcategories total exactly 100%.


Python Syntax Focus

The Engine: plt.pie(x) computes metrics automatically. Python sums the data list internally, determines each item’s relative fraction, and slices the 360-degree radius to match.
Key Argument: autopct='%1.1f%%' uses standard Python formatting string tokens to process and inject float percentage text labels right onto the graphic slices.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Statistics: Categorical nominal groups and their proportional value segments
budget_categories = ['Housing', 'Food', 'Utilities', 'Savings']
allocation_values = [40, 25, 15, 20]

# Initialization: Enforce 1:1 aspect ratio constraint to ensure a perfect circle shape
plt.figure(figsize=(5.5, 5.5))

# Execution: 'autopct' uses native string formatting tokens to render percentages inline
plt.pie(allocation_values, 
        labels=budget_categories, 
        autopct='%1.1f%%', 
        startangle=140, 
        colors=['#264653', '#2A9D8F', '#E9C46A', '#F4A261'])

# Decoration: Render structural layout elements
plt.title('Relative Proportion Composition Analysis', fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')

plt.show()

title: Data Visualization using Matplotlib
text:
Quick Reference Summary Table









Graph Model
Matplotlib Function
Core Statistical Purpose
Input Data Requirements




Standard Bar Chart
plt.bar(x, height)
Compares frequency counters across nominal groups
Two matched 1D arrays (Strings + Numbers)


Stacked Bar Chart
plt.bar(x, height, bottom)
Compares cumulative metrics while highlighting inner composition
Multiple numeric data vectors of equal length


Clustered Bar Chart
plt.bar(x_indices +/- offset, y)
Side-by-side group comparison without stacking tracking
NumPy sequence tracking keys + numeric series data


Line Chart
plt.plot(x, y)
Traces trends over continuous chronological scales
One sequential tracking key list + one numerical metric array


Histogram
plt.hist(x, bins)
Examines density shape and distribution skewness
One single continuous numerical array


Box Plot
plt.boxplot(x)
Maps five-number summary quartiles and flags extreme outliers
One single numerical data vector


Scatter Plot
plt.scatter(x, y)
Evaluates bivariate directional correlation patterns
Two matched numeric coordinate vectors


Pie Chart
plt.pie(x, labels)
Displays categorical ratios or compositions
One categorical array + one numeric value array

title: Data Visualization using Matplotlib
section: Syntax Translation: Python (Matplotlib) vs. R Equivalent
text:
Syntax Translation: Python (Matplotlib) vs. R Equivalent
The following comparison highlights structural mapping for our core plot models.








Visualization Concept
Python Syntax (Matplotlib)
R Equivalent Syntax (ggplot2)




Import Library
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
library(ggplot2)


Render Bar Chart
plt.bar(categories, values)
ggplot() + geom_col(aes(x, y))


Render Stacked Bar
plt.bar(x, y1); plt.bar(x, y2, bottom=y1)
ggplot() + geom_col(aes(x, y, fill=group))


Render Clustered Bar
plt.bar(indices-w, y1); plt.bar(indices+w, y2)
ggplot() + geom_col(aes(x, y, fill=group), position="dodge")


Render Line Chart
plt.plot(time, distribution)
ggplot() + geom_line(aes(time, distribution))


Render Histogram
plt.hist(data, bins=5)
ggplot() + geom_histogram(bins=5)


Render Box Plot
plt.boxplot(data)
ggplot() + geom_boxplot(aes(y=data))


Render Scatter Plot
plt.scatter(x, y, alpha=0.85)
ggplot() + geom_point(aes(x, y), alpha=0.85)


Render Pie Chart
plt.pie(values, labels=cats)
ggplot() + geom_bar() + coord_polar("y")


Set Canvas Size
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4.5))
options(repr.plot.width=7, repr.plot.height=4.5)


Apply Title
plt.title("Main Title")
labs(title = "Main Title")


Apply Axis Labels
plt.xlabel("X"), plt.ylabel("Y")
labs(x = "X", y = "Y")


Enable Gridlines
plt.grid(True)
(Enabled by default in ggplot2)


Display Window
plt.show()
(Implicitly auto-printed in R)

title: Mastering NumPy and Base R
text:
Data manipulation in Python is nearly synonymous with NumPy array manipulation: even newer tools like Pandas are built around the NumPy array. This section will present several examples using NumPy array manipulation. We’ll cover a few categories of basic array manipulations here:

Attributes of arrays

Determining the size, shape, memory consumption, and data types of arrays

Indexing of arrays

Getting and setting the value of individual array elements

Slicing of arrays

Getting and setting smaller subarrays within a larger array

Reshaping of arrays

Changing the shape of a given array

Joining and splitting of arrays

Combining multiple arrays into one, and splitting one array into many

title: Mastering NumPy and Base R
text:
4.1 One-dimensional subarrays

NumPy (Python)Base R




Code
x = np.arange(10)
x




Code
x[:5]   # first five elements




Code
x[5:]   # elements after index 5




Code
x[4:7]  # middle subarray




Code
x[::2]  # every other element






Code
x <- 0:9
x




Code
x[1:5]               # first five elements (1-based)




Code
x[6:length(x)]       # elements after position 5




Code
x[5:7]               # middle subarray




Code
x[seq(1, 10, by = 2)]  # every other element




Code
rev(x)               # all elements, reversed

title: Mastering NumPy and Base R
text:
4.2 Multidimensional subarrays
Multidimensional slices work in the same way, with multiple slices separated by commas.

NumPy (Python)Base R




Code
x2




Code
x2[:2, :3]   # two rows, three columns






Code
x2[1:2, 1:3]   # two rows, three columns




Code
x2[, 1]        # first column (all rows)




Code
x2[1, ]        # first row (all columns)

title: Mastering NumPy and Base R
text:
6.1 Concatenation of arrays
Concatenation, or joining of two arrays in NumPy, is primarily accomplished through the routines np.concatenate, np.vstack, and np.hstack. np.concatenate takes a tuple or list of arrays as its first argument.

NumPy (Python)Base R




Code
x = np.array([1, 2, 3])
y = np.array([3, 2, 1])
np.concatenate([x, y])


You can also concatenate more than two arrays at once:


Code
z = [99, 99, 99]
print(np.concatenate([x, y, z]))


np.concatenate can also be used for two-dimensional arrays:


Code
grid = np.array([[1, 2, 3],
                 [4, 5, 6]])

np.concatenate([grid, grid])






Code
x <- c(1, 2, 3)
y <- c(3, 2, 1)

c(x, y)




Code
z <- c(99, 99, 99)

c(x, y, z)




Code
grid <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3,
                 4, 5, 6),
               nrow = 2,
               byrow = TRUE)

rbind(grid, grid)




Code
cbind(grid, grid)

title: Data Manipulation with Pandas
text:
This chapter introduces advanced data manipulation techniques in Pandas. These techniques allow us to combine datasets, summarize grouped data, create multidimensional summaries, and efficiently process textual information.
The main topics covered are:

Merge & Join
Aggregation & GroupBy
Pivot Tables
Vectorized String Operations



Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

title: Data Manipulation with Pandas
text:
Introduction
Data are often stored across multiple tables. To perform meaningful analyses, we frequently need to combine these tables based on common variables. Pandas provides powerful relational operations through merge() and join().
The merge operation is conceptually similar to SQL joins.

title: Data Manipulation with Pandas
text:
Three Categories of Joins

One-to-One Joins
A one-to-one join occurs when each key value appears exactly once in both tables.


Code
df1 = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee": ["Bob", "Jake", "Lisa", "Sue"],
    "group": ["Accounting", "Engineering", "Engineering", "HR"]
})

df2 = pd.DataFrame({
    "employee": ["Lisa", "Bob", "Jake", "Sue"],
    "hire_date": [2004, 2008, 2012, 2014]
})

pd.merge(df1, df2)




Many-to-One Joins
A many-to-one join occurs when multiple observations in one table correspond to a single observation in another table.


Code
df3 = pd.DataFrame({
    "group": ["Accounting", "Engineering", "HR"],
    "supervisor": ["Carly", "Guido", "Steve"]
})

pd.merge(df1, df3)




Many-to-Many Joins
A many-to-many join occurs when duplicate keys exist in both tables.


Code
df4 = pd.DataFrame({
    "group": ["Accounting", "Accounting",
              "Engineering", "Engineering",
              "HR", "HR"],
    "skills": ["math", "spreadsheets",
               "coding", "linux",
               "communication", "recruiting"]
})

pd.merge(df1, df4)

title: Data Manipulation with Pandas
text:
Specifying the Merge Key

Using the Same Column Name
When both DataFrames contain a common column name, the key can be specified using the on parameter.


Code
pd.merge(df1, df2, on="employee")




Using Different Column Names


Code
df5 = pd.DataFrame({
    "name": ["Bob", "Jake", "Lisa", "Sue"],
    "salary": [50000, 70000, 80000, 60000]
})

pd.merge(
    df1,
    df5,
    left_on="employee",
    right_on="name"
)




Merging on Index


Code
df1a = df1.set_index("employee")
df2a = df2.set_index("employee")

pd.merge(
    df1a,
    df2a,
    left_index=True,
    right_index=True
)




Using .join()
The .join() method is a convenient shortcut for index-based merges.


Code
df1a.join(df2a)

title: Data Manipulation with Pandas
section: Set Arithmetic and the how Parameter
text:
Set Arithmetic and the how Parameter
The how parameter controls which keys appear in the merged result.

Inner Join
Keeps only keys that appear in both tables.


Code
pd.merge(df1, df2, how="inner")




Outer Join
Keeps all keys from both tables.


Code
pd.merge(df1, df2, how="outer")




Left Join
Keeps all keys from the left table.


Code
pd.merge(df1, df2, how="left")




Right Join
Keeps all keys from the right table.


Code
pd.merge(df1, df2, how="right")

title: Data Manipulation with Pandas
text:
Introduction
GroupBy is one of the most important tools in data analysis. It allows us to divide observations into groups and calculate summary statistics for each group.
The GroupBy operation follows the Split–Apply–Combine paradigm.

Split the data into groups.
Apply a function to each group.
Combine the results.

title: Data Manipulation with Pandas
text:
Sample Dataset


Code
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "key": ["A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "C"],
    "data1": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6],
    "data2": [7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12]
})

df

title: Data Manipulation with Pandas
text:
Split–Apply–Combine


Code
df.groupby("key").sum()

title: Data Manipulation with Pandas
text:
Aggregation Functions
Aggregation reduces each group to one or more summary values.

Mean


Code
df.groupby("key").mean()




Median


Code
df.groupby("key").median()




Multiple Aggregations


Code
df.groupby("key").aggregate(
    ["min", np.median, max]
)

title: Data Manipulation with Pandas
text:
Filtering Groups
Filtering removes groups that fail a specified condition.


Code
def filter_func(x):
    return x["data2"].std() > 1

df.groupby("key").filter(filter_func)

title: Data Manipulation with Pandas
text:
Transform
Transform returns a result having the same shape as the original dataset.


Code
df.groupby("key").transform(
    lambda x: x - x.mean()
)

title: Data Manipulation with Pandas
text:
Apply
Apply is the most flexible GroupBy operation.


Code
def normalize(group):
    group["data1"] = group["data1"] / group["data1"].sum()
    return group

df.groupby("key").apply(normalize)

title: Data Manipulation with Pandas
text:
Common Aggregation Methods



Method
Description




count()
Count non-missing values


sum()
Sum of values


mean()
Arithmetic mean


median()
Median


min()
Minimum value


max()
Maximum value

title: Python Modules and Packages
text:
A module is a file containing a set of code or a set of functions which can be included in an application. A module could be a file containing a single variable, a function, or a large codebase. This file has a .py extension.
Examples:

Random
Math
Statistics





A package is a directory containing collections of modules. The directory contains Python modules and also having __init__.py file by which the interpreter interprets it as a Package.
Examples:

Numpy
Pandas
Arch



Difference Between Module, Package, and Library

Module → A single Python file (.py )
Package → A folder containing multiple modules
Library → A collection of packages/modules providing functionality

More

title: Python Modules and Packages
text:
Modules

Creating a Module
To create a module, we write our code and save that in a file with.py extension.
Example: Let’s create a calc.py in which we define two functions, one add and another subtract.

# calc.py
def add(x, y):
    return (x+y)

def subtract(x, y):
    return (x-y)

This is all that is required to create a module.


Importing a Module
Modules can be used in another file using the import statement. When Python sees an import, it loads the module if it exists in the interpreter’s search path.
Example: Here, we are importing the calc that we created earlier to perform add operation.

import calc

print(calc.add(10,2))



Built-in Modules
Some common built-in Python modules include math, datetime, os, sys, random, statistics, collections, json, and re. These built-in modules can also be imported using the import statement, just as we did with the user-defined module above. Let us consider how functions are imported from a module.


Import Functions from a Module
01. Import One or More Specific Functions from a Module
This allows importing specific functions, classes, or variables rather than the whole module.

from random import randint, shuffle

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "mango", "orange"]

print(randint(5, 20)) # it returns a random integer number between [5, 20] inclusive

shuffle(fruits) # changes the order of elements in a list randomly
print(fruits)

print(choice(fruits)) # selects one random element from a given list


import random
from random import randint, shuffle

fruits = ["apple", "banana", "mango", "orange"]
print(random.choice(fruits))

Explanation: Only randint and shuffle are brought into the local namespace, so the prefix random. is not required for those functions. But when we use the choice function, it returns an error message without the prefix random.


02. Import All Functions
This imports everything from a module into the current namespace.

from statistics import * 

ages = [20, 20, 4, 24, 25, 22, 26, 20, 23, 22, 26]
print(mean(ages))       
print(median(ages))     
print(mode(ages))       
print(stdev(ages))      

Explanation: Every public name of statistics becomes directly accessible. To check what functions the module has got, we can use help(statistics), or dir(statistics).

import statistics

dir(statistics)



03. Import With Alias
When we import we can also rename the name of the function.

from math import pi as  P

print(P)

title: Python Modules and Packages
text:
Packages

Installing and Importing Packages
Unlike modules, packages cannot always be imported directly; they must first be installed.

import seaborn


!pip install seaborn

import seaborn


!pip install seaborn

import seaborn as sn
sn.lineplot(
    x=[1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
    y=[10, 15, 12, 18, 20]
)



Comparison with R








Feature
Python
R




Install package
pip install seaborn
install.packages("ggplot2")


Load package
import seaborn
library(ggplot2)


When install needed
Once
Once


When load needed
Every session
Every session


Keyword difference
import
library()









NoteImporting Functions



Importing functions from packages is the same as importing functions from modules. The syntax remains identical; only the source (module or package) changes.






Creating and Accessing Packages

Create a Directory: create a folder that will act as the package root.
Add Modules: Inside the directory, add Python files (modules). Each module can contain related functions or classes.
Add init.py: add an init.py file to the directory. This file tells Python that the directory should be treated as a package.
Create Sub-packages (Optional): One can organize code further by creating subdirectories with their own init.py files.
Import Modules: Modules or functions inside the package can be imported using dot notation, for example: from mypackage.module1 import greet

Example
In this example, we create a package called math_operations to organize mathematical functions. The package contains two modules:

basic: addition and subtraction
advanced: multiplication and division

First create a new folder named math_operations. Then create __init__.py file in the math_operations folder.

# __init__.py

from .basic import add, subtract
from .advanced import multiply, divide

Then create modules basic.py and advanced.py with following code:

# basic.py

def add(a, b):
    return a + b
  
def subtract(a, b):
    return a - b  


# advanced.py

def multiply(a, b):
    return a * b
  
def divide(a, b):
    return a / b

The folder structure of your package should look like this:
math_operations/
├── __init__.py
├── basic.py
└── advanced.py
Now we can import functions from the package and use them in our program.

from math_operations import add, subtract, multiply, divide

# Perform basic operations
print(add(5, 3))
print(subtract(10, 4))

# Perform advanced operations
print(multiply(4, 2))
print(divide(10, 2))

As you can see our package works perfectly.


Popular Python Packages \ Libraries for Data Analysis and Data Science
Python provides a rich ecosystem of packages for data analysis, statistical computing, and visualization. Some of the most widely used packages include:

NumPy – Provides support for numerical computing, multidimensional arrays, and mathematical operations.
Pandas – Offers data structures such as DataFrames for data manipulation, cleaning, and analysis.
Matplotlib – A comprehensive library for creating static, interactive, and animated visualizations.
Statsmodels – Provides tools for statistical modeling, hypothesis testing, regression analysis, and time series analysis.

These packages form the foundation of many data science workflows in Python and are conceptually similar to several commonly used R packages for data analysis and statistical modeling.
A simple comparison table could also be useful:



Area
Python Packages
R Packages




Numerical computing
NumPy
base R


Data manipulation
Pandas
dplyr


Data visualization
Matplotlib
ggplot2


Statistical modeling
Statsmodels
stats, forecast



The following sections provide a more detailed discussion of these packages, including their key features, common functions, and practical applications in data analysis and data science workflows.

title: Survival Analysis with statsmodels
text:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# LIBRARY IMPORTS
# numpy   → numerical operations (random number generation, exp)
# pandas  → tabular data (DataFrames), similar to data frames in R
# matplotlib / seaborn → plotting, similar to ggplot2 in R
# statsmodels → the main statistical library we are using today
#   - statsmodels.api (sm)          → array-based model interface
#   - statsmodels.formula.api (smf) → R-style formula interface e.g. "y ~ x"
#   - SurvfuncRight                 → Kaplan-Meier estimator for right-censored data
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.api as sm
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
from statsmodels.duration.survfunc import SurvfuncRight

# Plot aesthetics — equivalent to setting a ggplot2 theme in R
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", palette="muted")
plt.rcParams.update({"figure.dpi": 120, "axes.titlesize": 13})

title: Survival Analysis with statsmodels
text:
2.1 What is DeFi Lending?
Think of DeFi lending as a digital pawn shop. In a normal pawn shop, you bring in something valuable — say your watch — and the shop gives you cash. If you repay the cash plus interest, you get your watch back. If you don’t, the shop sells it to recover the money.
DeFi lending works the same way, except everything is automated by code on a blockchain. You deposit cryptocurrency as collateral — say $1,500 worth of Ethereum — and borrow $1,000 against it. The collateral ratio is 1.5, meaning you have put up 50% more than you borrowed. If the value of your Ethereum drops and that ratio falls below a safety threshold, the protocol automatically sells your collateral to recover the loan. That automatic sale is the liquidation event — the thing we are modelling the time-to in this analysis. A higher collateral ratio means you are a safer borrower with more of a buffer before the system acts.

title: Survival Analysis with statsmodels
text:
2.2 The Dataset
We use a synthetic dataset of 200 DeFi loans. Each row records how long a loan was observed (duration_days) and whether it was eventually liquidated (liquidated = 1) or is censored (liquidated = 0 — repaid or still active at the end of the 180-day observation window).

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# GENERATING SYNTHETIC SURVIVAL DATA
#
# The key difference from ordinary regression data is HOW we
# generate the outcome. We cannot just randomly assign event
# labels — we must generate a true event time for every loan
# from a survival distribution, then censor anything that falls
# outside the observation window.
#
# We use a Weibull distribution — the standard choice for
# survival times. In R this would be: rweibull(n, shape, scale)
# shape > 1 means the hazard increases over time (like aging —
# the longer a risky loan survives, the more likely it fails).
#
# Crucially, the scale parameter depends on the covariates:
#   higher collateral_ratio → larger scale → longer survival
#   Variable rate           → smaller scale → shorter survival
# This ensures the Cox model later has a real signal to detect.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

np.random.seed(42)
n_loans = 200

# Covariates
# np.random.normal() is equivalent to rnorm() in R
# np.random.choice() is equivalent to sample() in R
collateral_ratio  = np.random.normal(loc=1.5, scale=0.3, size=n_loans).clip(1.0, 3.0).round(2)
rate_type         = np.random.choice(["Stable", "Variable"], size=n_loans, p=[0.5, 0.5])

# Encode rate_type numerically for the linear predictor
# (1 = Variable, 0 = Stable) — equivalent to as.integer() in R
rate_type_numeric = (rate_type == "Variable").astype(float)

# Linear predictor: combines both covariates into a single score
# Higher score → larger Weibull scale → longer survival time
linear_predictor  = 0.8 * collateral_ratio - 0.4 * rate_type_numeric
scale_param       = np.exp(linear_predictor) * 100
shape_param       = 1.5  # hazard increases over time

# True (latent) time until liquidation for every loan
# np.random.weibull() is equivalent to rweibull() in R
true_event_time = np.random.weibull(shape_param, size=n_loans) * scale_param

# Apply the 180-day observation window
# Loans not liquidated by day 180 are right-censored (liquidated = 0)
# Equivalent to: ifelse(true_event_time > 180, 0, 1) in R
observation_window = 180
observed_time = np.minimum(true_event_time, observation_window).round(1)
liquidated    = (true_event_time <= observation_window).astype(int)

defi_loans = pd.DataFrame({
    "duration_days":    observed_time,
    "liquidated":       liquidated,
    "collateral_ratio": collateral_ratio,
    "rate_type":        rate_type
})

defi_loans.head()


# Value counts — equivalent to table() in R
# Shows how many loans were liquidated vs. censored
defi_loans["liquidated"].value_counts().rename({0: "Censored (Active/Repaid)", 1: "Liquidated"})

title: Survival Analysis with statsmodels
text:
3.1 The Kaplan–Meier Survival Curve
The Kaplan–Meier estimator calculates the probability of surviving (avoiding liquidation) past a given time \(t\). It is entirely non-parametric — it makes no assumptions about the shape of the survival distribution.
\[
\hat{S}(t) = \prod_{t_i \leq t} \left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right)
\]
where \(d_i\) is the number of events and \(n_i\) is the number of loans still at risk at time \(t_i\).
We use SurvfuncRight from statsmodels, which takes the time column first and the event status column second, and automatically accounts for censored observations.

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# KAPLAN-MEIER ESTIMATOR
#
# SurvfuncRight() is statsmodels' Kaplan-Meier implementation.
# Arguments:
#   [0] time column   → how long was each loan observed?
#   [1] status column → did liquidation occur? (1 = yes, 0 = censored)
#
# Once fitted, the object stores:
#   .surv_times → the time points where the curve steps down
#   .surv_prob  → the survival probability at each step
#
# We plot using ax.step() directly rather than sf.plot() —
# this gives us full control over colours and styling, and
# avoids a bug in sf.plot() where confidence bands are drawn
# as separate lines that are difficult to colour correctly.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

sf = SurvfuncRight(defi_loans["duration_days"], defi_loans["liquidated"])

fig, ax = plt.subplots()

ax.step(sf.surv_times, sf.surv_prob, where="post",
        color="#4C72B0", linewidth=2, label="Survival estimate")

# Confidence interval — stored as a (lower, upper) array inside the object
ci = sf.simultaneous_cb()
ax.fill_between(sf.surv_times, ci[0], ci[1],
                step="post", alpha=0.15, color="#4C72B0", label="95% CI")

ax.set_xlabel("Days since Loan Origination")
ax.set_ylabel("Probability of Surviving (Not Liquidated)")
ax.set_title("Kaplan–Meier Survival Curve for DeFi Loans")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.05)
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Survival Analysis with statsmodels
text:
3.2 Stratified Survival Curves
Does the rate type (Stable vs Variable) affect survival probability? We fit a separate Kaplan–Meier curve for each group and overlay them. This is the Python equivalent of survfit(Surv(time, status) ~ group) in R’s survival package.

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# STRATIFIED KAPLAN-MEIER
#
# We loop over each category of rate_type, filter the DataFrame
# to that subset (equivalent to filter() in R's dplyr), and fit
# a separate SurvfuncRight object for each group.
#
# We plot each group's .surv_times and .surv_prob using ax.step()
# with a distinct colour per group — giving an unambiguous,
# correctly coloured plot for both curves.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
colors = {"Stable": "#4C72B0", "Variable": "#DD4444"}

for rate in ["Stable", "Variable"]:

    # Filter rows — equivalent to defi_loans[defi_loans$rate_type == rate, ] in R
    subset = defi_loans[defi_loans["rate_type"] == rate]

    # Fit a separate KM estimator for this group
    sf_group = SurvfuncRight(subset["duration_days"], subset["liquidated"])

    # Plot using the stored attributes directly
    ax.step(sf_group.surv_times, sf_group.surv_prob,
            where="post", color=colors[rate], linewidth=2, label=rate)

ax.set_xlabel("Days since Origination")
ax.set_ylabel("Survival Probability")
ax.set_title("Kaplan–Meier Survival Curve by Rate Type")
ax.legend(title="Rate Type")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.05)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Survival Analysis with statsmodels
text:
4.1 Model Specification
Kaplan–Meier is great for visualising, but it cannot handle continuous predictors or control for multiple variables simultaneously. For that we use the Cox Proportional Hazards model, which models the hazard rate \(h(t)\) — the instantaneous risk of liquidation at time \(t\), given survival up to that point.
\[
h(t \mid X) = h_0(t) \exp(\beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2)
\]
\(h_0(t)\) is the baseline hazard, left completely unspecified — this is what makes Cox semi-parametric. The predictors shift that baseline multiplicatively.

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# COX PROPORTIONAL HAZARDS MODEL
#
# smf.phreg() uses the same R-style formula interface as lm() in R.
# Key arguments:
#   formula → "outcome ~ predictors" (same syntax as R's coxph)
#   data    → the pandas DataFrame
#   status  → the binary event column (1 = liquidated, 0 = censored)
#             This replaces Surv(time, status) from R
#
# C(rate_type) tells statsmodels to treat rate_type as a
# categorical variable — equivalent to factor() in R.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

model_ph = smf.phreg(
    formula="duration_days ~ collateral_ratio + C(rate_type)",
    data=defi_loans,
    status=defi_loans["liquidated"]
).fit()

print(model_ph.summary())

title: Survival Analysis with statsmodels
text:
4.2 Interpreting Hazard Ratios
Just as logistic regression uses Odds Ratios, survival analysis uses Hazard Ratios (HR). We obtain them by exponentiating the raw coefficients — because the model is on the log-hazard scale. This works the same way as exp(coef(model)) in R.

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# COMPUTING HAZARD RATIOS
#
# model_ph.params stores the coefficients on the log-hazard scale.
# np.exp() converts them to Hazard Ratios — the same operation as
# exp(coef(model)) in R.
#
# model_ph.conf_int() returns a 2-column array [lower, upper].
# We exponentiate both bounds to get the CI on the HR scale,
# equivalent to exp(confint(model)) in R.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

ci_cox = model_ph.conf_int()

hazard_ratios = pd.DataFrame({
    "Hazard Ratio (HR)": np.exp(model_ph.params),
    "2.5%":              np.exp(ci_cox[:, 0]),
    "97.5%":             np.exp(ci_cox[:, 1]),
    "p-value":           model_ph.pvalues
}).round(3)

hazard_ratios

How to read this table:







Term
Interpretation




HR > 1
Increases the hazard — liquidation happens faster


HR < 1
Decreases the hazard — protective effect


collateral_ratio
HR < 1: higher collateral lowers the daily liquidation risk


C(rate_type)[T.Variable]
Compares Variable to Stable (the reference). HR > 1 means Variable rate loans face a higher liquidation hazard




If the 95% CI crosses 1.0, the effect is not statistically significant — just as a CI crossing 0 on the coefficient scale would be in R.
